summary: Measure whether a strong CLAUDE.md makes Claude Code (Opus) use rdbg vs a neutral note

summary.verdict: The strong prompt produced a maximal shift in rdbg adoption: it flipped usage from 0/5 in control to 5/5 in strong (0% to 100%), with a mean of 7.6 rdbg calls per run versus 0 in control. This came at a steep cost. Mean token usage nearly tripled (135,067 to 385,734, ~2.85x) and mean wall time roughly tripled (~24s to ~67s), since the forceful mandate drove the agent into extended debugger-driven investigation loops. Crucially, pass rate was 5/5 in both conditions, so the strong prompt bought no correctness improvement on this task; the failing test was already reliably fixable without the debugger. Net: the strong prompt is highly effective at changing tool-adoption behavior but, on this problem, purchases that behavior change purely at the expense of tokens and latency with no accuracy payoff.

workflowProgress.label: run:strong-1
workflowProgress.promptPreview:
Run EXACTLY this one command and wait for it to finish (it launches a headless Claude Code session on a debugging task, ~1-6 min). It prints a single JSON object on its last stdout line. Return that JSON's fields verbatim — do not run anything else, do not interpret.

    cd /Users/mohsen/code/rust-debugger-skill/benchmarks/adopt && PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" python3 run_one.py strong 1

workflowProgress.label: synthesize
workflowProgress.promptPreview: These are 10 runs of Claude Code (Opus, medium effort) fixing the SAME failing Rust test, with rdbg available. 'strong' = a forceful CLAUDE.md mandating rdbg and discouraging grep/read loops; 'control' = a neutral one-line note. Compute, per condition: adoption rate (how many used_rdbg=true out of N, e.g. "5/5"), mean rdbg_calls, mean tokens, pass rate. Then a one-paragraph verdict on how much the…
